lines.text: Chapter six, section four. Real world applications.
Everything we have worked through in the Ace-to-Five Game has direct implications for how to play real poker, and this section draws out those connections explicitly.
There are two major themes: first, learning to target specific hands in your opponent's range, and second, learning to construct ranges that force your opponent into genuinely difficult decisions.
Let's start with targeting.
One of the most common errors that poker players make — especially out of position with medium-strength hands — is reasoning something like this: 'I'm not sure I'm ahead, so I'd better check.'
The problem with this thought process is not just that it is incomplete — it is that it is driven by fear rather than by strategic calculation.
It focuses on avoiding a loss rather than on maximizing expected value.

And critically, it doesn't resolve what to do after checking — if your opponent bets, you're back to making another fear-based decision: are you more afraid of losing to a better hand, or more afraid of being bluffed?
Consider what we learned about Opal holding a Jack in the Ace-to-Five Game.
At equilibrium, she is indifferent between betting and checking — both options produce the same expected value.
But in neither case is she acting out of fear or uncertainty.
When she bets, she has a specific target: the Tens, Nines, Eights, and Sevens in Ivan's range — the hands that will face a close call and might make the wrong decision.
When she checks, she has a different specific target: the Tens, Sixes, and Fives that Ivan might bet into her.
The reason to check a Jack is not that Ivan might have a better hand — after all, at equilibrium Opal even checks Aces sometimes.
The reason to check is to create a situation where Ivan has incentive to bet with hands that Opal can beat.
That is a completely different frame than fear-based reasoning, and it leads to completely different decisions.
Now notice something crucial: whether Opal bets an Ace or bets a Jack, she is targeting the same group of hands — the Tens, Nines, Eights, and Sevens in Ivan's range.
Those are the threshold hands, the ones where Ivan's decision is genuinely close.
The Aces, Kings, and Queens in Ivan's range are trivial — he calls automatically and there is nothing Opal can do to affect that outcome.
The Fives and Sixes in Ivan's range are also trivial — he folds automatically no matter what Opal holds.
The only hands Opal can actually influence with her bet are the ones right at the decision boundary.
This is a foundational principle: focus your strategic thinking on threshold hands, because those are the hands where your action actually changes the outcome.
Everything else is noise.
When playing exploitatively, this targeting framework becomes even more powerful.
Once you have identified which hands in your opponent's range are the relevant targets, you can think about what kind of mistake he is most likely to make with those specific hands.
If your opponent tends to pay off with bluff-catchers even when bet into, then betting becomes more profitable and you should value bet more aggressively.
If your opponent bluffs too frequently after you check, then checking and calling becomes the better line.

If your opponent is passive both ways — unwilling to bluff and unwilling to call with bluff-catchers — then checking and folding might actually be correct, because you'll often get to showdown for free and win with your medium-strength hand.
Now let's talk about the second major theme: constructing ranges that put your opponent to difficult decisions.
A single hand cannot do this on its own.
The difficulty of your opponent's decision depends not on the specific card you hold, but on the entire range of hands you might hold in that spot.
When Opal bets a Jack, Ivan has a difficult time with a Nine because of the possibility that Opal might be bluffing with a Five or Six.
When Opal checks, Ivan has a difficult time deciding whether to bet a Ten because Opal might be checking a Nine that pays him off, but she also might be trapping with a Jack or better.
Neither of these decisions would be difficult if Opal were a simpler, more predictable player who always bet her strong hands and never mixed.
The difficulty is produced by the range, not by any individual hand.
This has an important implication for how to plan your strategy before the action begins.
The book lays out a clear sequence of reasoning you should apply when constructing your betting range from any given position.
Start by determining how many of your strong hands you need to check, because you need a strong enough checking range to keep your opponent from profitably making thin value bets after you check.
The number of strong hands you check is calibrated to make your opponent indifferent to value betting his threshold hands — the Tens in the Ace-to-Five Game, the marginal top pairs in real hold 'em.
Once you have set aside the strong hands that belong in your checking range, the rest become available for value betting.
That gives you your value betting range.
Then look at the pot odds your bet will offer — how many bluffs you need relative to your value bets to keep opponents indifferent to calling with their bluff-catchers.
For a half-pot bet, the ratio is three value hands for every one bluff.

Fill your bluffing range starting from the very bottom of your hand spectrum — the absolute weakest hands you can hold — and work upward only if you have exhausted those hands and still need more bluffs.

This disciplined approach to range construction serves a dual purpose: it ensures you are not over-bluffing or under-bluffing, and it gives you the context to make the right decision with any individual hand you happen to hold.
Many players are reluctant to bet strong hands because they worry it will signal strength.

But if you are also betting weak hands as bluffs at the right frequency, your bet communicates nothing about the specific hand you hold — it only tells your opponent that you have a range that includes both strong hands and weak hands, which he already knew.
The strategic invisibility of individual hands within a properly constructed range is one of the most powerful concepts in this entire approach to poker.
There is a practical concession to reality here as well.
In a live game, you will rarely have the time or mental bandwidth to enumerate every combination of value bets and bluffs precisely.
What you can do is make a quick estimate: how weak is your current hand relative to the weakest hands you could hold in this situation?
Is it one of the very worst hands in your range, or will you sometimes have even weaker holdings?
If it is the worst or among the worst, it is a bluffing candidate.
Also ask yourself: roughly how wide is your value betting range in this spot?
A wider value betting range can support more bluffs, so if you are in a spot where you can bet many strong hands for value, you have more room to bluff with your weaker hands.
A narrow value betting range means you should restrict bluffing to only the absolute best bluffing candidates.
Thinking through this quickly before you act is far better than not thinking about it at all.
It will not produce perfectly balanced ranges, but it will prevent the large, easily exploitable mistakes — bluffing wildly or never bluffing — that cost players real money.
There is one more practical point worth dwelling on.
When you are playing exploitatively, this planning process actually gets easier, not harder, because you can simplify by taking certain considerations off the table.
If you know your opponent calls too much with bluff-catchers, you no longer need to bluff — the bluffing part of the calculation disappears.
If you know your opponent will not make thin value bets after you check, you do not need to trap with strong hands, so you can bet your entire strong range and simplify your value betting decision.

Exploitative play is not about abandoning the logic of game theory — it is about applying that logic with the added input of real information about a specific opponent, which allows you to simplify and tighten your decision making in ways that produce higher expected value.
